title: Quantum control of spin qubits using SOT-driven nanomagnets
authors: Aniruddha Chakraborty, Kanishk Modi, Dhritiman Bhattacharya, Uditendu Mukhopadhyay, Suddhasatta Mahapatra, Jayasimha Atulasimha
abstract: Spin rotation (SR) is an essential capability for realization of single and two-qubit gates in spin quantum computing (SQC) architectures. To perform SR, resonant AC magnetic fields are either generated by microwave current pulses fed to an antenna, or voltage pulses applied to a gate, in presence of an inhomogeneous Zeeman field. While the former approach is limited by gate-speed and site-selectivity of SR, the latter adds to the decoherence of the spin qubits. Here, we propose an alternative technique for driving high-speed SR without compromising the qubit coherence, by employing spin-orbit-torque (SOT)-driven nanomagnets to produce oscillating magnetic fields, locally at the qubit site. The proposed scheme is highly energy-efficient, scalable, and compatible with the CMOS fabrication technology.
summary: This paper proposes using spin-orbit-torque (SOT)-driven nanomagnets to generate localized, oscillating magnetic fields for controlling spin qubits. By simulating the system dynamics, the authors demonstrate that this method achieves fast gate operations and long coherence times, offering a highly scalable alternative to current quantum control techniques.
main_problem: The core challenge is achieving fast, high-fidelity spin rotation (SR) for quantum gates in spin qubits while minimizing decoherence caused by external magnetic fields.
main_result: The proposed technique using SOT-driven nanomagnets generates highly localized, oscillating magnetic fields that enable fast Rabi frequencies (e.g., 52.25 MHz) and maintain long coherence times ($T_2^* \sim 40-250 \mu s$), rivaling or exceeding existing methods.
method: The authors simulate the system dynamics using micromagnetics (LLG equation) to model the SOT-driven magnetization precession, which generates the oscillating stray B-field used to drive the qubit.
model_or_system: The system involves spin qubits in semiconductor quantum dots controlled by oscillating magnetic fields generated by SOT-driven nanomagnets (Co-Ni/Pt/W). The control mechanism relies on the Spin-Hall effect generating a torque on the nanomagnet's magnetization.
key_observables: Rabi frequency ($f_R$), coherence time ($T_2^*$), and the spatial profile of the oscillating magnetic field.
important_parameters: RF charge current ($I_c = 200 \mu	ext{A}$), nanomagnet dimensions (e.g., 100 nm $	imes$ 80 nm), and the target ESR frequency ($\approx 5.6$ GHz).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes that the primary decoherence source is related to the gradient of the static stray field, and that higher harmonics of the oscillating field have negligible impact on fidelity.
figures_summary: Figures compare Rabi oscillations showing high fidelity (99.99%) under resonant driving, and plot $T_2^*$ and decoherence rate contributions ($\Gamma_X, \Gamma_Y$) based on the placement of multiple nanomagnets.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by first establishing the need for better SR control, detailing the SOT-driven mechanism via micromagnetics simulations, and finally quantifying the performance by calculating Rabi frequencies and coherence times ($T_2^*$) compared to established methods like EDSR.
why_interesting: This work directly addresses the physical implementation challenges of solid-state quantum computing by proposing a scalable, CMOS-compatible method for coherent qubit manipulation using spintronics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: (a) Schematic representation of magnetization dynamics in the NM. B0 is the externally applied magnetic field, and the magnetization of the NM ( ⃗M) is shown precessing around ⃗B0. (b) Schematic representation of the spin qubit device, showing the gate layers, the SOT strip-line, the NM, and the QD hosting the spin qubit in the strained-Si QW of the Si/SiGe heterostructure. The NM is positioned directly above the QD, 90 nm above the Si-QW plane.

FIG. 2: (a) The three components of the oscillating magnetic field generated by the precessing magnetization of the NM. (b) The frequency components of the Bz component, with the higher harmonic enlarged in the inset.

FIG. 3: Color-maps of various qubit parameters at the qubit plane. (a) Calculated Rabi frequency at the qubit- site, plotted for a region encompassing the footprint of the entire NM (shown by the dotted ellipse). (b) Plot of T ∗ 2 = (Γx + Γy)−1 over a smaller region, according to Eq. 2. Plots of the decoherence rate (Γx, Γy) contribution from gradient of Zeeman field along (c) X- and (d) Y- axes respectively.

FIG. 1: Rabi oscillations driven by the resonant stray fields originating from the nanomagnet, located at (0,0,90) nm with respect to the qubit location, exhibit a gate fidelity of 99.99%, whereas the non-resonant drive (green) shows considerably lower fidelity (94%).

FIG. 2: Figures show the results for the case of 3 NMs placed next to each other along their major axis with a gap of 20 nm. (a) Plot of T ∗ 2 . Plots of the decoherence rate contribution from gradient of Zeeman field along (b) X- and (c) Y- axes.

Spintronics.note: The new paper directly uses SOT-driven nanomagnets to generate oscillating magnetic fields for spin qubit control, which falls squarely into the domain of coupling quantum-optics concepts (qubits) with spintronics (SOT-driven magnets).
